Okay, so I'm trying to figure out whether the mutant code change in DateTimeZone.java at line 111 is causing any issues. The mutant changed some conditions around lines 108-114.

Looking at the original code, it seems like there's a condition checking if zone is null and then returns getDefault(). Then, it checks if the id equals "UTC" and returns UTC if so. After that, it initializes dtz to null and gets the convertedId.

The mutant modified line 111 by replacing `id.equals("UTC")` with `true`. So now, regardless of the id, it will always return DateTimeZone.UTC when this condition is met. That could cause problems because if the id isn't "UTC", it might incorrectly return UTC instead of proceeding to other checks.

Looking at the failing tests, several tests in TestDateTimeZoneCutover are still failing after the mutation. This suggests that changing line 111 might have introduced a bug because some time zone conversions aren't handled correctly anymore. For example, tests related to US Central and Australian Eastern Time (ausNSW) are still failing, which makes sense if the code is returning UTC when it shouldn't.

On the other hand, TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear started passing after the mutation. That's probably because the bug in that test was fixed by this change, but I'm not entirely sure how these changes are related.

I think the mutant might be causing issues because line 111 is now always true, which skips necessary checks for other time zones and could lead to incorrect DateTimeZone instances being returned. This would explain why several tests are still failing—they rely on proper handling of different time zones beyond just UTC.
</think>

The mutant in question modifies the condition at line 111 from checking if `id.equals("UTC")` to always evaluating as true, which can cause incorrect DateTimeZone returns for non-UTC ids. This leads to test failures in multiple cases where expected time zones aren't properly handled.
